topics.title: Safety First
topics.description: LOTO, PPE, and test before touch
topics.intro: Safety is not a chapter you skip. These rules keep you alive on day one.

cards.title: Treat every wire as hot
body: Never assume a circuit is off because someone said so or because a switch is down.
Verify with your meter every time before you touch.
cards.remember: If you did not test it, it is still live.

cards.title: Lockout / Tagout (LOTO)
body: Shut off power at the breaker or disconnect.
Lock it so nobody can turn it back on.
Tag it with your name and why it is locked.
Test with a meter to prove zero energy.
cards.remember: Test before touch — every job, every time.

cards.title: Live → Dead → Live
cards.body: Before checking a dead circuit: test your meter on a known live source, test the circuit, then test the live source again. This proves your meter and leads work.

cards.title: PPE you actually wear
cards.remember: PPE only works if it is on your body.

cards.title: Never work live (as a new apprentice)
body: Working energized is for trained, authorized people on specific tasks.
Your job is to de-energize, lock out, and verify.
cards.remember: Say: I am not comfortable working it hot.

cards.title: Panel doors and clearances
body: Keep panel doors closed when you are not working inside.
NEC 110.26: about 3 ft deep, 30 in wide, 6.5 ft tall working space — never block panels with storage.
cards.remember: Neat work and clear space keep everyone safe.

topics.title: How Power Gets to a Building
topics.description: From the utility to your lights and appliances
topics.intro: Follow the path of electricity from the pole to the outlet. When you see the whole picture, troubleshooting gets easier.

cards.title: The big picture
body: Utility transformer sends power to the building.
Service drop or underground feed goes to the meter.
Meter measures usage, then power enters the main panel.
Branch circuits feed lights, outlets, and big appliances.

image.alt: Residential and commercial power flow diagrams
image.caption: Tap to zoom — residential (top) and commercial (bottom).

cards.remember: Power always has a path: in on the hots, back on the neutral, safety on the ground.

cards.title: Residential path step by step
list: Main breaker panel (neutral and ground bonded here only)
cards.remember: The main panel is the first disconnect — neutral and ground bond only here.

cards.title: What the main breaker does
body: It shuts off all power to the house in one move.
Individual breakers only shut off their circuit.
cards.remember: Main off = whole house dark.

cards.title: 120V vs 240V at home

rows: Dryer, range, water heater, large AC

cards.remember: Two hots plus neutral give you both 120V and 240V.

cards.title: Common home circuits
cards.remember: Breaker protects the wire — not the appliance.

cards.title: Commercial power flow (simple)
body: Utility sends 3-phase power to a meter section and main switchboard.
Distribution panels feed lighting, kitchen equipment, and HVAC.
Large motors often use 480V from an MCC (motor control center).
cards.remember: Commercial has more voltage flavors: 120, 208, 277, and 480.

cards.title: Fast food restaurant example
cards.remember: Follow the one-line in your head: utility → meter → main → panels → loads.

topics.title: Single-Phase vs Three-Phase & Colors
topics.intro: Know what system you are on and which color is which phase. Interviewers ask this a lot.

cards.title: Residential = single-phase
body: 120/240V single-phase for houses, apartments, and small shops.
Two hots (often black and red), one neutral (white), one ground (green or bare).
cards.remember: Home = single-phase 120/240V.

cards.title: Commercial often = three-phase
body: 120/208V wye — offices, restaurants, retail.
277/480V wye — warehouses, big HVAC, industrial lighting.
cards.remember: More phases = more power for big equipment.

cards.title: 120/208V wire colors — BRB
cards.remember: BRB = Black, Red, Blue.

cards.title: 277/480V wire colors — BOY
cards.remember: BOY = Brown, Orange, Yellow.

cards.title: Residential conductor colors
cards.remember: White is neutral — not a hot unless code allows a marked exception.

cards.title: Voltage cheat sheet
cards.remember: Read the nameplate — voltage tells the truth.

topics.title: Panels: Main vs Sub
topics.intro: The #1 apprentice mistake is bonding neutral and ground together in a subpanel. Learn the rule once and never forget it.

cards.title: Main service panel
list: Neutral and ground are bonded together here only
cards.remember: Main panel = bond neutral and ground.

cards.title: Subpanel rules
list: Fed from main with 4 wires: 2 hots, neutral, ground
Ground bar bonded to the enclosure
No bond screw between neutral and ground
cards.body: NEVER bond neutral and ground in a subpanel. You create parallel paths and ghost trips.

cards.title: Bonding cheat card
cards.remember: Main bonds. Sub floats neutral.

cards.title: Landing feeder wires — safe order
body: With power locked out: ground first, neutral second, hots last.
Keeps your tools away from live lugs while you work.
cards.remember: Ground → neutral → hots.

cards.title: Subpanel feed example
body: Garage, workshop, or EV subpanel needs L1, L2, neutral, and ground.
Neutrals land only on the isolated neutral bar.
Grounds only on the ground bar tied to the can.
cards.remember: Four wires in — neutrals and grounds separated in the sub.

cards.title: Disconnect switch & MCC (know the names)
body: Disconnect: local shutoff for HVAC, motors, equipment service.
MCC: industrial cabinet with starters, VFDs, breakers, overloads.
cards.remember: Disconnect = local off. MCC = motor neighborhood.

topics.title: Wire Sizes & Breakers
topics.intro: The breaker protects the wire. Size the wire first, then pick the breaker.

cards.title: Memory trick: 14-20-30-50-65-100
cards.remember: Breaker size must not exceed wire ampacity.

cards.title: Why this matters
body: If the breaker is too big for the wire, the wire can overheat before the breaker trips.
That is a fire risk. Never use whatever wire is on the truck without checking.
cards.remember: Breaker protects wire — not the toaster.

cards.title: Common wire types
cards.remember: Wire type must match where and how it is installed.

cards.title: Voltage drop (simple rule)
body: Long runs lose voltage. Keep branch drop under about 3%, total under about 5%.
If a run is over 100 ft, consider going up one wire size — cheaper than a callback.
cards.remember: Long run? Bigger wire.

cards.title: Continuous loads
body: Loads that run 3+ hours (heat, some motors) may need 125% when sizing.
For interviews: know that a 20A breaker is not always 20A of load forever.
cards.remember: Volts × amps = watts. Size for load and duty.

cards.title: Box fill (why boxes matter)
body: Each box has a max number of wires based on volume.
Count conductors, clamps, and devices — too many wires fails inspection.
cards.remember: Stuffing a box is not neat — it is a fail.

topics.title: Multimeter Mastery
topics.intro: Your meter is the most important tool in your bag. Learn the ports, the settings, and where to put the probes.

cards.title: What a multimeter measures
list: Current (A) — in series or use a clamp meter

image.caption: Tap to zoom — probe placement A through L.

cards.remember: Wrong setting + live circuit = damaged meter or injury.

cards.title: Meter ports

rows: High current only — then move back

cards.remember: Black to COM. Red to VΩ unless you are doing high amps in series.

cards.title: NEVER do this
list: Measure voltage on the amp port
Measure resistance or continuity on a live circuit
Move leads while touching live parts
Hold metal probe tips with your fingers
cards.body: These mistakes blow meters and hurt people.

cards.title: Test a 120V outlet
body: Dial: AC voltage (V~). Black to neutral (wide slot), red to hot (narrow slot).
Expect about 110–125V. Hot to ground should also read ~120V.
Neutral to ground should be near 0V.
cards.remember: Hot-neutral ~120. Neutral-ground ~0.

cards.title: Troubleshooting trick: open neutral
body: If hot-to-neutral reads 0 but hot-to-ground reads 120V, you may have an open neutral.
This is a common real-world problem — not magic.
cards.remember: Weird voltages mean follow the path — do not guess.

cards.title: Test 240V
body: Hot 1 to hot 2: expect ~240V.
Each hot to ground: expect ~120V each.
cards.remember: 240V is hot-to-hot. Each leg is still 120V to ground.

cards.title: Continuity test
body: Power OFF. Dial to continuity. Beep = complete path.
Use on switches, fuses, and wire ends.
cards.remember: No beep = open (broken). Beep = good path.

cards.title: Current — use a clamp when you can
body: Series amperage requires breaking the circuit — apprentices often blow meters here.
Clamp meters read amps around a single conductor — safer and faster.
cards.remember: Clamp around one wire — not both hots together.

cards.title: Poster: multimeter section
cards.body: Zoom the field poster multimeter quadrant for dial symbols and safety reminders.
cards.remember: Live → dead → live before you trust any reading.

topics.title: Conduit Bending (EMT)
topics.intro: Clean bends look professional and save rework. EMT is the most common commercial raceway.

cards.title: Common bends

rows: Go over a pipe or obstacle

cards.remember: Measure twice, bend once.

cards.title: Bender parts
cards.remember: Line up the arrow with your mark.

cards.title: Stub-up 90 with take-up
body: Measure stub height wanted. Subtract take-up. Mark conduit at that distance.
Align arrow with mark. Bend to 90°.
cards.remember: 24" stub on 1/2" EMT: 24 − 5 = mark at 19".

cards.title: Offset multipliers
body: Obstacle height × multiplier = distance between marks.
Example: 4" obstacle at 30° → 4 × 2 = 8" between marks.
cards.remember: 30° offset is the fast field favorite (×2).

cards.title: Common bending mistakes
list: Kinked EMT from poor foot pressure
cards.remember: Keep conduit straight in the bender plane.

cards.title: Support rules (typical)
body: Support within 3 ft of a box and about every 10 ft along the run (check NEC for your install).
Good work is level, parallel, and symmetrical.
cards.remember: Ugly bends get ripped out — take your time.

topics.title: NEC Essentials
topics.intro: You do not memorize the whole NEC. You learn where to look and the big rules interviewers expect.

cards.title: What the NEC is for
body: NFPA 70 — practical safeguarding of people and property.
It is not a design manual. It sets minimum safe rules.
cards.remember: Code = minimum safe. Good craftsmen go beyond sloppy minimums.

cards.title: Top articles to know by name
cards.remember: Article 250 is the safety superstar.

cards.title: Article 250 — grounding & bonding
list: Service neutral bonding only at main
cards.remember: Grounding connects to earth. Bonding connects metal to clear faults fast.

cards.title: Article 110 — working space
body: 110.26: about 3 ft deep, 30 in wide, 6.5 ft tall in front of panels.
110.14: torque terminations — use a torque screwdriver on lugs.
cards.remember: Never block a panel with boxes or shelves.

cards.title: Article 300 — wiring methods
list: Same circuit conductors in same raceway
6 inches minimum free conductor in boxes (300.14)
cards.remember: Protection from physical damage — nail plates on studs.

cards.title: How to answer code questions in an interview
body: Say you use the index and tables — speed finding matters.
Admit when you do not know, but know which article you would open.
cards.remember: Honest + safe beats pretending to know everything.

topics.title: GFCI, AFCI, Devices & Switches
topics.intro: Know what device does what job, where it is required, and how to wire a receptacle and switch.

cards.title: GFCI — protects people
body: Trips on small leakage current (around 4–6 mA).
Required near water: baths, kitchens, garages, outdoors, and more per 210.8.
cards.remember: GFCI = shock protection near water.

cards.title: AFCI — protects against fire
body: Detects dangerous arcing.
Required in many dwelling living areas per 210.12.
cards.remember: AFCI = arc / fire protection in living spaces.

cards.title: Device wiring colors
cards.remember: Brass hot, silver neutral, green ground.

cards.title: Pigtail neutrals
body: Do not depend on the device to carry neutral downstream.
Pigtail neutrals in the box — pros do it every time.
cards.remember: Pigtail the neutral — do not chain through the device.

cards.title: 3-way switches
body: Two switches control one light from two places (stairs, halls).
Uses traveler wires between 3-ways plus line and load.
cards.remember: 3-way = two locations, one light.

cards.title: 4-way switches
body: Goes between two 3-ways when you need three or more switch locations.
cards.remember: 4-way = middle switch between two 3-ways.

cards.title: Receptacle spacing (residential basics)
body: 210.52: walls — no point more than 6 ft from a receptacle (12 ft max apart).
Countertops: within 2 ft of edge; gaps matter on islands too.
cards.remember: 6 ft rule on walls — learn it before layout.

topics.title: Commercial & Industrial Intro
topics.description: Motors, MCCs, PLCs, and bigger voltage
topics.intro: After residential basics, commercial and industrial work adds 3-phase gear and controls. Learn the words first.

cards.title: Commercial wiring methods
cards.remember: EMT indoors, MC branches, rigid where toughness matters.

cards.title: T-bar ceiling work
body: You will pull MC, EMT, lights, and often data in grid ceilings.
Keep runs parallel and supported — GCs and inspectors notice.
cards.remember: Ceiling work = neat parallel runs.

cards.title: Motors — basics
list: Overload sized to FLA on nameplate
Motor breakers can be larger than wire for inrush (Article 430)
cards.remember: Read the motor nameplate — FLA is your friend.

cards.title: VFD and PLC (know the names)
body: VFD: variable frequency drive — controls motor speed. Very common.
PLC: programmable logic controller — automation brain for conveyors, pumps, plants.
cards.remember: Controls and automation pay well later — learn terms now.

cards.title: Pull boxes
body: Needed when conduit runs are too long or have too many bends.
Pulling without boxes burns time and wire.
cards.remember: Too many bends? Add a pull box.

cards.title: Arc flash respect
cards.body: 480V systems can kill instantly. Wear required PPE, respect labels, and never work outside your training.

topics.title: Tools & Jobsite Habits
topics.description: What to carry and how to act
topics.intro: Interviewers care more about reliability and hustle than genius-level code memory.

cards.title: First-year tools to own
cards.remember: Show up with tools — borrow less, learn more.

cards.title: First-year rules — ALWAYS
list: Ask questions once, then do the work
cards.remember: Hustle and cleanup get you invited back.

cards.title: First-year rules — NEVER
list: Work live without training and permission
cards.remember: Honesty about what you do not know builds trust.

cards.title: Daily 2-minute drill
list: Photo anything weird for the foreman
cards.remember: Plan like rocket science, work smooth, test like you are paranoid.

cards.title: Magic phrases that save you
list: I need to verify that with the foreman.
Can you show me on the print?
I am not comfortable working it hot.
cards.remember: Nobody got fired for being too safe.

cards.title: Print marking system
cards.remember: Photo every redline — that is your as-built backup.

topics.title: Interview & Career
topics.intro: You are not expected to know everything. You are expected to be safe, reliable, and eager to learn.

cards.title: What interviewers actually want
cards.remember: Attitude beats memorizing the whole NEC.

cards.title: Why electrician?
body: I like working with my hands, solving problems, and learning a skilled trade with long-term growth.
cards.remember: Keep answers short and real.

cards.title: What do you know already?
body: I study NEC basics — grounding and bonding, residential vs commercial, conduit, and safety. I verify every circuit before I touch it.
cards.remember: Mention test-before-touch — it marks you as safe.

cards.title: Low-voltage background (if that is you)
body: I have pulled cable, terminated, followed paths, and kept work neat for testing.
I am ready to apply that discipline to higher voltage and learn the NEC on the job.
cards.remember: Neat low-voltage work proves you can learn high-voltage right.

cards.title: Five concepts that sound smart
cards.remember: Five solid topics beat fifty vague ones.

cards.title: Fastest way to earn respect
cards.remember: Respect on site is earned with actions, not talk.

cards.title: High-value skills for years 2–4
cards.remember: Specialize later — master safety and basics first.

topics.title: Quick Reference Tables
topics.intro: Bookmark this topic in your head for field days — wire, colors, and formulas in one place.

cards.title: Ohm's law & power
cards.remember: Volts push, amps flow, ohms resist.

cards.title: Grounding vs bonding

rows: Connection to earth — stabilize voltage, surges
Metal connected together — fault path to trip breaker

cards.remember: They are not the same thing — interviewers trap you here.

cards.title: Conduit fill — fist rule (#12 THHN)
cards.remember: Overfill = hard pull, hot wire, failed inspection.

cards.title: Multimeter readings (AC)
cards.remember: Voltage tells the truth. Prints lie sometimes.

cards.title: Abbreviations
cards.remember: Learn the language — it speeds up every conversation on site.

cards.title: Apprentice quick memory — three worlds
cards.remember: Know which world you are in before you touch anything.

cards.title: Master's bottom line
body: Power does not care how smart you sound.
It cares how tight, how big, and how right your neutrals and grounds are.
cards.remember: Test before touch. Every time.
